title: Bank buffers look thin as confessions loom
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Companies and Markets Bank buffers look thin as confessions loom The Australian Financial Review James Eyers 1 May 2026 813 words English AFNR 18 Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. Major banks' bad debt buffers may not be as strong as they seem, with loan distress set to increase amid soaring fuel costs and rising interest rates, as three of the big four prepare to deliver interim results that could show emerging pressure on their balance sheets. That's the view of analysts at Macquarie, who argue that changes in how banks model risk mean the provisions they hold to guard against bad debts could be up to $1 billion lower than in previous economic downturns. "We believe sector provisioning is not as robust as it first appears," Macquarie analyst Carlos Cacho said in a report this week. The level of bank buffers - which protect shareholders and depositors if financial distress causes customers to default on their loans - is in focus after Westpac and National Australia Bank boosted collective provisions earlier this month. Both banks cited business customers' exposure to surging energy prices, after the Middle East conflict closed the Strait of Hormuz through which about 25 per cent of global oil supply passes. ANZ is also expected to lift its provisions for impaired loans on Friday, when it reports a half-year cash profit of around $3.7 billion. NAB will report on Monday, and Westpac on Tuesday, the same day as the Reserve Bank of Australia's latest decision on the cash rate. Nuno Matos, ANZ's chief executive, is part-way through a major simplification program to reduce costs at ANZ while improving customer service. He now faces a deteriorating economic outlook, with more retail customers facing cost-of-living pressures as inflation continues to rise. The latest bank results come at a time when the prudential regulator is watching asset quality across the sector like a hawk. "Recent trading updates from Westpac, NAB, and Judo show banks are rebuilding collective provisions via economic overlays, signalling a less benign credit outlook," UBS analyst John Storey said this week. Investors will want to be reassured that loan book stress is not emerging and will scrutinise banks' assumptions about the economy's direction. "Are we entering a more permanent earnings downside cycle - especially within the context of bank valuations which are still well above longer-term averages?" Storey asked. Macquarie's Cacho said banks may need to lift provisions even higher - especially if economic conditions continue to deteriorate. Regulatory changes have allowed banks to lift their common equity tier 1 capital ratios - a measure of the amount of high-quality capital they hold as a buffer to absorb financial shocks - by between 80 and 160 basis points over the past seven years, Cacho noted, and when provisions are adjusted for such "optimisation", they would be 16 basis points lower than reported. If banks had to lift provisions to the same level as the second half of 2020, when the pandemic put borrowers under pressure, they would increase between $400 million and $1 billion from current levels, reducing 2026 full-year earnings by 3 per cent to 9 per cent, according to Macquarie's analysis. Some investors, citing the more challenging environment, have already sold down bank shares. "ANZ may be the next to increase provisioning, but the bigger question in our mind is around investor appetite and sentiment for the banks," said Romano Sala Tenna, a portfolio manager at Katana Asset Management. "We have seen consistent retail selling over the past six months, and we have closed out all major bank positions, except Macquarie." The banks are trading above historical valuations and are exposed to mounting stress among consumers and businesses amid a deteriorating economy, he added. With annual core inflation rising to 3.5 per cent in March, well above the RBA target range, most economists predict the central bank will raise the cash rate again at its meeting next week, after two hikes already this year. Westpac reckons there will be another three rate rises by the end of the year. If the cash rate rose by 0.25 of a percentage point in each of May, June and August, the repayments on a $600,000 loan would rise by approximately $3300 a year. Including the two rate rises this year, total annual repayments would be almost $5500 higher by August. Bank bosses say they're acutely aware that the economic situation will make it tougher for borrowers while savers benefit from higher rates, creating problems for intergenerational equity. "When we look at cost of living, the impact of interest rates going from 4 per cent to potentially 6 per cent or 7 per cent at some stage, as a banker, you need to think about ... the true impact that is going to have to the large majority of Australia," ING chief executive Melanie Evans told a Trans-Tasman Business Circle and Australian Banking Association lunch this week. summary: Major Australian banks may face increased loan distress as bad debt buffers appear weaker than previously thought, according to analysts at Macquarie. With rising fuel costs and interest rates, banks are expected to report higher provisions for impaired loans, potentially impacting their earnings. Investors are closely monitoring the banks' balance sheets amid concerns over consumer and business stress in a deteriorating economic environment.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Analysts suggest banks' bad debt buffers could be up to $1 billion lower than in past downturns.
Rising interest rates and inflation are increasing cost-of-living pressures on borrowers.
Investors are concerned about the banks' valuations and potential earnings declines due to higher provisioning.
relevance: Understanding the financial health of banks and their provisioning strategies is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing risk and investment opportunities.
